coredata.dc:title: Functionalized silicon nanoparticles as fluorescent probe for detection of hypochlorite in water
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Taheri, M.
Mansour, N.
coredata.dc:description: In this work amine functionalized silicon nanoparticles colloid is utilized to prepare a fluorescent probe for detecting low concentration levels of hypochlorite in water. The sensor is prepared by adding organosilane in silicon nanoparticles colloidal solution which freshly synthesized by laser ablation of silicon target in distilled water. The amine-terminated surface of silicon nanoparticles is confirmed using Fourier transform infrared, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy methods. The photoluminescence measurements of the functionalized silicon nanoparticles colloid exhibit high photostability and good reproducibility making them ideal fluorescent probes for sensing applications. It shows high sensitivity, excellent selectivity and rapid response (˜6 min) toward hypochlorite. The room temperature fluorescent sensor detects hypochlorite of water in the linear concentration range from 1 to 5 μM with a 0.2 μM detection limit. The sensor properties including high sensitivity, fast response and facile synthesis of the amine-functionalized silicon nanoparticles are compared with other reports in literature for fluorescent detection of hypochlorite in water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence sensor
Hypochlorite detection
Silicon nanoparticle
Amine surface functionalization

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the detection of ClO− based on functionalized Si NPs.

(a) FTIR spectrum, (b) EDX analytical graph, (c) XRD pattern, (d) SEM image, (e) TEM image (inset: Histograms of the nanoparticles size distributions) and (f) DLS histogram of the amine-functionalized Si NPs.
(a) Uv–vis absorption, (b) PLE and (c) PL spectra of the functionalized Si NPs.

(a) PL spectra of the probe, (b) PL intensity of the probe in different pH values, (c) Photostability of the probe and (d)Fluorescence response of the probe in the presence of various ions (with excitation/emission peaks located at 405/490 nm).

(a) PL spectra of the functionalized Si NPs in the presence of different ClO− concentrations (b) Plot of the relative fluorescence intensity (I0-I)/I0 versus the concentration of ClO−, the inset is the linear section of the plot (with excitation/emission peaks located at 405/490 nm).

Plot of I/I0 versus increasing reaction time after addition of 10 μM ClO− into the amine-functionalized Si NPs (I0 and I correspond to the PL intensity at 490 nm in absence and presence of ClO−, respectively.
Comparison of our results with other reports for fluorescent detection of ClO− in water.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: DV 26, Disperse Violet 26; CDs-RhB, Carbon Dots and Rhodamine B.

head.ce:title: Functionalized silicon nanoparticles as fluorescent probe for detection of hypochlorite in water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Shahid Beheshti University, G. C., Evin, Tehran, 19839, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Shahid Beheshti University, G. C., Evin, Tehran 19839, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work amine functionalized silicon nanoparticles colloid is utilized to prepare a fluorescent probe for detecting low concentration levels of hypochlorite in water. The sensor is prepared by adding organosilane in silicon nanoparticles colloidal solution which freshly synthesized by laser ablation of silicon target in distilled water. The amine-terminated surface of silicon nanoparticles is confirmed using Fourier transform infrared, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy methods. The photoluminescence measurements of the functionalized silicon nanoparticles colloid exhibit high photostability and good reproducibility making them ideal fluorescent probes for sensing applications. It shows high sensitivity, excellent selectivity and rapid response (˜6 min) toward hypochlorite. The room temperature fluorescent sensor detects hypochlorite of water in the linear concentration range from 1 to 5 μM with a 0.2 μM detection limit. The sensor properties including high sensitivity, fast response and facile synthesis of the amine-functionalized silicon nanoparticles are compared with other reports in literature for fluorescent detection of hypochlorite in water.

ce:section.ce:para: Hypochlorite’s (ClO−) antimicrobial activities, oxidizing properties, reasonable persistence in water, ease of use, solubility in water, relative stability, no poisonous residuals, no color and low cost make it feasible to be widely used as a bleaching agent and disinfectant in our daily life and many manufacturing processes [1,2]. Our life has been enriched by ClO−, but ClO− is harmful to the health of humans and animals if the concentration exceeds a certain amount. On the other hand, ClO− can cause damages by reacting with biological molecules [3]. The human immune system in response to infection can generate minute quantities of hypochlorite. However, the excessive production of ClO− can cause a variety of diseases such as cardiovascular problems, inflammatory diseases, lung injury, renal disease and some cancers [4]. Therefore, development of practical platforms to the selective and quantitative determination of ClO− is of great importance for human health.

Various methods have been developed to detect ClO−, such as colorimetric [1,4], electrochemical [5], chemiluminescence [6] and fluorescent methods [7–10]. Among those reported various methods, fluorescent probe detection is a promising method for detecting ClO− due to high sensitivity, better selectivity, fast response, less cell damage and simplicity [2,11–13]. So far, many fluorescent probes for recognition and detection of ClO−, including organic molecule [4,14] and quantum dots [2,7,8] have been devoted. Efforts to enhance sensitivity, water solubility, photostability and signal intensities of fluorescent probes performance, based on quantum dots (QDs) such as carbon dots [7,15,16] and silicon dots [2,17] are still under investigations.

The presence of amine-end groups on silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs) surface can enhance stability of nanoparticles in biological environment and in a simple way it allows the attachment of biomolecules [18]. Various methods for forming amine terminated Si NPs have been reported by different groups. For instance, Rosso-Vasic and coworkers have reported synthesis of amine terminated Si NPs with three different alkyl chain lengths between the Si core and the NH2 moiety. For this purpose, they synthesized hydrogen terminated Si NPs in reverse micelles and functionalized them with three different amine-terminated w-alkenes: allylamine, hex-5-enylamine and undex-10-en-1-amine, in the presence of a catalytic amount of H2PtCl6 [19]. Shiohara et. al. have studied the difference between platinum catalyst capping and UV-irradiation capping of Si QDs. The results exhibit that the surface modification of Si QDs with UV-capping is optimal compared to the platinum catalyst [20]. Recently, the surface modification of freshly prepared Si NPs via condensation of hydrolyzed 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) has been investigated as an emerging type of strong fluorescent nanomaterial [18]. There are several methods available for synthesis of Si NPs, in which the laser ablation method is most commonly used. The main advantages of this technique include the green synthesis without chemical agents, the production of wide range of nanoparticles, a possibility to work at ambient conditions, its versatility that allows in situ manipulations and its high production yield [18].

In this study, functionalized silicon nanoparticle with good photostability is synthesized for selective and sensitive detection of ClO− in aqueous solution. For this purpose, the freshly prepared Si NPs immersed in hydrolyzed APTEs aqueous solution at room temperature. The optical and sensing properties are characterized by UV–vis absorption spectrometry, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), and photoluminescence (PL) measurements methods. We show that the sensing behavior of the synthesized amine-functionalized Si NPs for detecting the ClO− is very effective with good response time. Further, the possible sensing mechanism of amine-terminated Si NPs has been discussed and presented. Fig. 1 shows a basic principle of the functionalized Si NPs-based ClO- sensor.

The functionalized Si NPs are fabricated using the following steps. First, preparation of a suspension of the Si NPs in to distilled water by nanosecond pulsed laser ablation of the n-type silicon wafer [21]. To this end, the cleaned Si wafer is immersed in a glass vessel filled with 10 mL of the distilled water and irradiated with the first harmonic of a Q-switched Nd:YAG laser operated at 1064 nm. The laser pulse energy is 100 mJ with repetition rate of 10 Hz and pulse duration of 20 ns. The laser beam is crossed on the target from the top, and the optical path in water is about 5 mm. The solution is continuously stirred with a magnetic stirrer during the ablation process to disperse the produced Si NPs. In addition, the target is moved by a micro translational stage during the laser ablation process. The ablation process is continued for 5 min at room temperature and ambient pressure. Under our experimental conditions, the estimated production yield of the Si NPs is about 0.8 mg/h. Consequently the colloidal solution contains about 0.007 g/L of Si NPs. Then, solution of amine-functionalized Si NPs is obtained by adding APTES directly in the freshly laser synthesized Si NPs colloidal solutions. In this way, 1 mL of Si NPs colloidal and 0.1 mL of APTES are dissolved in 30 mL deionized water. The mixture is sonicated for 2 h at room temperature. The concentration of amine-functionalized Si NPs is calculated to be about 15 g/L by weighing the mass of dried it.

FTIR spectroscopy is utilized to identify the functional groups present in Si NPs. FTIR spectroscopy of colloidal is obtained on a BRUKER-VECTOR 22 FTIR spectrometer. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements are carried out using a Stoe-Stadv X-ray diffractometer with CuKα radiation in the 2θ range (10–80) with Brentano configuration. Investigations of morphological properties of the functionalized Si NPs have been studied by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The SEM images and energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analyses are acquired on a Phillips XL30. Also, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the nanoparticles is acquired on a Zeiss-EM10C microscope working at an acceleration voltage of 100 KeV. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements of the amine-functionalized Si NPs is carried out at room temperature using a Nanophox 90–246 V equipped with a He-Ne laser. Solution pH measurements are performed using a Hanna HI 2211 pH meter. The absorption spectra of the colloidal are recorded at room temperature on a Perkin Elmer LAMBDA 25 UV–vis spectrometer. The photoluminescence and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) measurements of colloidal solution are carried out on a Perkin Elmer LS-45 fluorescence spectrophotometer. To investigate nanosensors fluorescent properties, the micro cuvette is positioned on a CUV-ALL-UV 4-way cuvette holder, which couples to a 600 μm-core diameter optical fiber. The micro cuvette containing the nanosensor solution is illuminated transversely by a blue-violet laser diode, at a wavelength of 405 nm. The nanosensor emission is directed on to an Ocean Optics USB 2000+ spectrometer via the optical fiber. In a typical ClO− detection procedure, 100 μL of phosphate buffer (50 mM, pH 6) is mixed with 50 μL of the amine-functionalized Si NPs. Then, different concentrations of ClO− solutions are added in to above mixture. The mixture is diluted with water to final volume of 1 mL. The mixed solution is incubated for 7 min and the PL spectra are recorded at room temperature.
FTIR spectra of the amine-functionalized Si NPs colloids are measured and shown in Fig. 2 (a). The results exhibit absorption peaks ranging 600–4000 cm−1.

The peak at 3310 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching mode of hydrogen in Si−OH groups [22]. A smaller peak is observed at 2380 cm−1, which is attributed to C–H bonds [21]. Similarly, the peaks at the region 2350–1950 cm−1 correspond to the stretching monohybrids and coupled Si-H vibration [23,24]. The peak at 1684 cm−1 is assigned to the NH2 deformation modes of the amine groups [18]. Weak peak around 1084 cm−1 is associated to Si-O vibration modes which it may be resulted from the reaction between the fresh active surface of the Si NPs and water during the laser ablation [25]. Fig. 2(b) shows the EDX analysis of the amine-functionalized Si NPs. The EDX analyses indicate that C, N, O and Si atoms make up the primary chemical composition of the functionalized Si NPs and there are no other impurities in the sample. This analysis further confirmed that the Si NPs successfully functionalized with amine groups. Fig. 2 (c) depicts the X-ray diffraction pattern of amine-functionalized Si NPs exhibiting a diamond crystalline structure with well-resolved diffraction peaks for the silicon (111) plane. The highly intense peak at 2Ө = 28.5° indicates the existence of crystalline silicon in the sample. The morphology of the Si NPs colloidal solution after injection of APTES is studied by SEM and TEM images presented in Fig. 2(d) and (e), respectively. The typical TEM image for amine-functionalized Si NPs showed that the shape of this particle is somewhat spherical with a diameter about 5 nm. The size information of the amine-functionalized Si NPs is obtained using DLS data to compare with the size measured by TEM image. Particle size distribution of the amine-functionalized Si NPs is analyzed by DLS histogram indicates average particles size about 6 nm, as shown in Fig. 2(f). This is in good agreement with the size measured by TEM images.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of amine-functionalized Si NPs. As can be seen, the spectrum consists of two distinct bands: a strong band centered at 303 nm and a broad absorption below around 400 nm. This strong and broad absorption allowing excitation over a long wavelength range [26]. The PLE spectrum of amine-functionalized Si NPs is examined to gain more insight on the emitting mechanism of sample. Fig. 3(b) shows the PLE spectra of the amine-functionalized Si NPs with the detection energy of 2.75 eV (450 nm). The PLE spectrum has a maximum at about 385 nm (3.22 eV). The PLE peak position at 385 nm is possibly associated with the direction transition at Γ point (Γ25΄→Γ15) (energy is 3.46 eV for bulk Si). Possibly the quantum confinement effects (QCEs) is attributed to the red shift of direct transition at Γ25΄→Γ15 by 0.24 eV from the value of bulk Si [27]. Fig. 3(c) indicates the room temperature PL spectra of amine-functionalized Si NPs at different applied excitation wavelength ranging from 385 to 425 nm. The highest emission intensity, centered at about 450 nm, is obtained at the excitation wavelength of 385 nm. By increasing the excitation wavelength, the emission band shows red-shift and its peak intensity also decreases. This can be attributed to the size distribution of the silicon nanoparticles. Therefore, the QCEs is a convenient mechanism for PL of the amine-functionalized Si NPs at excitation wavelengths range of 385 to 425 nm. Photoluminescence quantum yield of the amine-functionalized Si NPs in the visible region is obtained using the comparative method [3,28]. The PL quantum yield of the amine Si NPs is estimated to be 9–10% using coumarin 500 dissolved in 2-methylpentane as the standard, whose quantum yield is regarded as 77% [29].

A 405 nm laser diode is used as excitation source for investigating optimize different detection conditions, photostability and selectivity the fluorescent probe. The maximal PL peak of amine-functionalized Si NPs is located at 490 nm, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). Investigation of the pH effect on the nanosensor is of great importance. The fluorescence response of the amine-functionalized Si NPs in aqueous solution with different pH values is shown in Fig. 4(b). The amine-functionalized Si NPs indicated the strongest PL intensity at pH 6, thus we chose sodium phosphate buffer pH 6 for the following experiments. The amine-functionalized Si NPs possesses excellent photostability for over 30 min under ambient conditions, which is shown in Fig. 4(c). I0 and I denote peak of PL intensity at the initial irradiation time and at time t, respectively. The PL intensity of the amine-functionalized Si NPs decreased by 15% after continuous irradiation for 30 min. The selectivity of the fluorescence probe is evaluated in the presence of various ions as shown in Fig. 4(d). I0 and I correspond to PL intensity of the amine-functionalized Si NPs at 490 nm in the absence and presence of different ions, respectively. The 20 μM of ClO− and other ions with 200 μM concentration are added into the amine-functionalized Si NPs colloidal solution, and the PL response of each solution is recorded. The competitive ions indicated little influence on the fluorescence intensity of the amine-functionalized Si NPs even at a concentration 10 times higher than that of ClO−. The results show that the amine-functionalized Si NPs are highly specific for ClO−.

The sensitivity of probe for the detection of ClO− is investigated by adding different concentrations of ClO− into solutions of the amine-functionalized Si NPs. The fluorescence response of the probe with additions of different ClO− concentrations is shown in Fig. 5 (a). The fluorescence intensities of the amine-functionalized Si NPs at 490 nm decreased gradually with increasing concentrations of ClO− from 1 to 180 μM. It can be seen that when the ClO− concentrations is higher than 20 μM, the fluorescence intensities of probe no longer decreased much. The Stern-Volmer equation is used to quantify the quenching mechanism as follows [8,30]:
(1)
I 0/I=K SV[Q]+1
where K SV is the quenching constant of ClO−, [Q] is the concentration of ClO−, I 0 and I denote the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of ClO−, respectively. A good linear behavior and a detection limit (DL) of 0.20 μM (with a signal to noise ratio of 3) is observed in the ClO− concentration range of 1–5 μM with correlation coefficient of 0.995, as shown in Fig. 5(b). The value of K SV is calculated to be 4.4 × 104 M-1 based on linear regression of the plots.

Our results show that the fluorescence of the amine-functionalized Si NPs probe is quenched in the presence of ClO−. It is shown that the fluorescence quenching of the detection system is via a dynamic quenching mechanism [2]. The dynamic quenching process fundamentally depends on electron transfer mechanism, which is known as redox mechanism. It is well known that the ClO− is strong oxidizing agents [8]. As a result, the fluorescence quenching mechanism may be attributed to the electron transfer from the probe to ClO−, as shown in Fig. 1. Therefore, the fluorescence of the amine-functionalized Si NPs probe is effectively quenched by ClO−.

The detection time of probe is another important factor for designing ClO− nanosensors. Therefore, the response time of the amine-functionalized Si NPs toward ClO− is also studied. The dynamic responses (PL intensity versus time) of the amine-functionalized Si NPs in the presence of 10 μM ClO−, is shown in Fig. 6 . The fluorescence intensity of the amine-functionalized Si NPs decreases slowly over time and does not change after about 6 min. This result indicates that the amine-functionalized Si NPs could be acts a rapid probe for monitoring ClO−.

In order to check the sensitivity of the amine-functionalized Si NPs our results are compared with several reports in recent years and are summarized in Table 1 . Compared with these methods, the fluorescent ClO− nanosensor in this work has shown some advantages and novelties. First, the detection limit of this method is much lower than some methods [8,31,32]. Second, the response time of the amine-functionalized Si NPs is shorter than some methods [2]. In addition, the nanosensor in this work shows good reproducibility, high sensitivity and remarkable photostability. The results of this work indicate that the performance of the Si NPs nanosensors is significantly improved by surface functionalization through the amine groups.

In this study, the amine-functionalized silicon nanoparticle fluorescence sensor with good photostability is synthesized for selective and sensitive detection of ClO− in water. The sensor optical and sensing properties are characterized by UV–vis absorption spectrometry, FTIR, and PL measurements methods. Our results indicate that ClO− is able to selectively quench the fluorescence of amine-functionalized Si NPs through the electron transfer mechanism. The amine-functionalized Si NPs is utilized as nanosensor for ClO- with sensing properties such as rapid response (˜6 min), good reproducibility, high sensitivity and remarkable photostability. This sensor can remarkably detect as low as 0.20 μM concentrations of ClO− in water. The biocompatible Si nanostructure containing amine functional group is prepared using a green protocol with the outstanding optical properties that may be used in many applications in imaging and biosensing of ClO− in living cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Saeed Javadi Anaghizi in the Center Laboratory of the Shahid Beheshti University for providing EDX analysis.
